program_outcomes: An ability to identify, formulate, and solve complex engineering problems by applying principles of engineering, science, and mathematics

An ability to apply engineering design to produce solutions that meet specified needs with consideration of public health, safety, and welfare, as well as global, cultural, social, environmental, and economic factors.
An ability to communicate effectively with a range of audiences.

An ability to recognize ethical and professional responsibilities in engineering situations and make informed judgments, which must consider the impact of engineering solutions in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts.
An ability to function effectively on a team whose members together provide leadership, create a collaborative and inclusive environment, establish goals, plan tasks, and meet objectives.
An ability to develop and conduct appropriate experimentation, analyze and interpret data, and use engineering judgment to draw conclusions.
An ability to acquire and apply new knowledge as needed, using appropriate learning strategies.
Knowledge of probability and statistics, including applications appropriate to computer engineering.
Knowledge of mathematics through differential and integral calculus and basic sciences.

Knowledge of engineering topics including computing science necessary to analyze and design complex electrical and electronic devices, software, and systems containing hardware and software components.

courses.name: Introduction to Computer Engineering
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with literacy in computers and data processing and to help them become computer users either as programmers or as users of application software packages.
courses.description: The course covers computer hardware, computer software, applications, the computer industry, and social issues in a weekly lecture component. It also includes laboratory, workshop, and recitation components in which students follow one of two alternatives chosen by their department: programming in a high-level language such as Pascal, Fortran, C, Java, or Python, or the use of application software packages such as word processing, spreadsheets, and data management tools.

courses.name: Digital Systems
courses.description: Number systems. Boolean algebra, logic networks and simplification. Logic design with gates. MSI and LSI technologies. Combinational and sequential circuits. Counters, shifters, computer organization, arithmetic logic, memory and control units, mini and microcomputer systems. Laboratory experiments and applications of digital logic design.

courses.name: Introduction to Object Oriented Programming
courses.description: Programming methodology: specification, design, coding, program correctness. Review of data types: scalar types, structured types. Data structures: linear structures (stacks, queues, linear lists), non-linear structures (trees, sets), files (sequential, random access). Implementation of data structures: linked list implementation, multidimensional array implementation, record implementation, string, stack, queue, tree and set implementations. Recursion.

courses.name: Discrete Computational Structures
courses.description: Propositional logic and proofs. Set theory. Functions and relations. Algebraic structures. Groups and semi-groups. Graphs, lattices and Boolean algebra.

courses.name: System Programming
courses.description: Overview of compilers, interpreters, assemblers, linkers and loaders. Unix environment and system calls. Shell programming. Signals and exceptions. Localization and Unicode. PERL and CGI programming. Assembly language programming. Introduction to multithreading. Introductory Graphical User Interface (GUI) programming.

courses.name: Data Structures and Algorithms
courses.description: Graphs. Advanced sorting. Hashing. Stack structures. Search structures. Complexity. Parallel algorithms. File organization.

courses.name: Introduction to Database Systems
courses.description: Logical organization of data: entity-relationship modeling of data. Hierarchical, network and relational models. Data definition and query languages. Normal forms and database design. Physical design and access strategies. Security, integrity and reliability. Design and implementation of a simple database management system including file security and a simple query language.

courses.name: Computer Organization
courses.description: RTL design. Basic computer organization. Processor instruction sets and their implementation. Addressing techniques. ALU, hardware and microprogrammed controllers. Memory units. I/O structures and interrupt handling. Improvements in the Von Neumann machine. Datapath structures. Assembly programming.
learning_outcomes: Recognize the history of computer design and recent developments in the field
Understand processor instruction execution through RISC-V assembly programming
Understand design principles and challenges related to modern processors
Understand design principles and challenges related to hierarchical memory systems
Understand design criteria such as performance, power efficiency, cost, and security for computers

courses.name: Algorithm Analysis
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the main paradigms of algorithm analysis, the methods and mathematical tools used to analyze algorithm performance, and the theory of parallel algorithms. It also aims to familiarize students with known sequential and parallel algorithmic solutions to commonly encountered problems.
courses.description: Analysis of computer science algorithms, including sorting, searching, paging, and parallelism. Analysis of mathematical algorithms, including games and puzzles, network algorithms, and probabilistic algorithms.
learning_outcomes: Analyze the main paradigms of algorithm analysis.
Use methods and mathematical tools to analyze the performance of algorithms.
Understand the theory of parallel algorithms.
Recognize known sequential and parallel algorithmic solutions to commonly encountered problems.

Study complexity analysis theory, common techniques for performance analysis, and basic algorithm classes such as comparison-based, recursive, divide and conquer, dynamic, greedy, numerical, and graph algorithms.
Learn lower bound theory.
Examine parallel architectures and parallel algorithms in detail.
Be introduced to mathematical tools such as interpolation and the master theorem.
Study probabilistic algorithms as an important and rapidly growing research area.

courses.name: Operating Systems
courses.description: Evolution of operating systems. Concepts of multiprogramming and time-sharing. User and program interfaces. Concurrent processes, CPU scheduling, process synchronization, critical section problem. Deadlock prevention, avoidance, detection and recovery. Memory management, swapping, multiprogramming with fixed and variable partitions. Paging, segmentation, virtual memory, page replacement algorithms. File system structures, allocation methods, directory implementations. I/O interfaces, secondary storage structure. Protection and security: access matrix and rights, capabilities, security issues. Linux case studies.

courses.name: Introduction to Probability and Statistics for Computer Engineers
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide a solid foundation in probability theory and statistics for computer engineering students.
courses.description: Introduction to probability theory. Random variables, expected value, variance, and moment generating functions. Distributions: Bernoulli, binomial, uniform, Gaussian, exponential, Poisson, gamma. Introduction to statistical concepts. Sampling and sample statistics. Point and interval estimation. Hypothesis testing. Regression. Numerical and computational aspects of random variable generation, sampling, and estimation.
learning_outcomes: Understanding the laws and applications of probability theory
Using Bayes' theorem
Understanding the concept of random variables
Understanding probability distributions and their properties
Understanding the concept of sampling
Understanding the concept of hypothesis testing
Understanding the concept of regression
Understanding some practical applications of probability and statistics

courses.name: Embedded Systems Design Fundamentals
courses.description: Embedded system design flow. Hardware-software co-design. Real-time software design. Real-time debugging techniques and tools. Hardware-software integration: interface design. Examples on the design of data acquisition and control systems. Laboratory.
learning_outcomes: Distinguish an embedded computing system from a general-purpose computing system.
Design an embedded system using software and hardware components.
Apply embedded software design techniques for interaction with sensors, actuators, and other devices.
Explain and implement timed behavior.

courses.name: Principles of Programming Languages
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the major programming paradigms and the languages that represent them. It also aims to provide a conceptual foundation for understanding programming language structures and implementation techniques rather than teaching a single specific language.
courses.description: This course covers the syntax and semantics of programming languages, common language constructs such as control structures and subprograms, and major programming paradigms including functional and logic programming. Various programming languages are used to illustrate fundamental concepts, structures, and paradigms.
learning_outcomes: Define the fundamental concepts and structures of programming languages.
Understand different programming paradigms and their applications.
Evaluate and choose the most suitable programming paradigm for different problems.
Write programs using more than one programming paradigm.
Understand how a computer program works.

courses.name: Formal Languages and Automata Theory
courses.description: Strings and languages. Classification of grammars. Finite state automata and regular expressions. Pushdown automata and context-free grammars. Context-sensitive grammars and linear bounded automata. Turing machines.

courses.name: Software Engineering Project Design
courses.description: Management and implementation of a large software development effort as a group. Application of software engineering principles in a project environment.
learning_outcomes: Experience applying software development techniques and methodologies in various stages of the software development life cycle, including planning, requirements gathering, design, implementation, testing, deployment, and delivery.
Acquire team-based software development skills such as communication, planning, coordination, and time management.
Gain the communication skills necessary for interaction with customers and project team members.
Develop skills in planning and managing software development projects.
Gain the ability to use software development tools, techniques, environments, and methodologies for effective and efficient software development.
Apply various Computer Engineering knowledge and algorithms learned in previous courses to software development.

courses.name: Introduction to Signal Processing for Computer Engineers
courses.description: Introduction to discrete-time and continuous-time signals and systems with computer engineering applications. Time-domain signal representations, impulse response of linear time-invariant systems, convolution, Fourier series, spectral representation of signals, Fourier transform, digital signals and sampling, reconstruction, filtering, Z-transform representation, discrete Fourier transform, signal processing algorithms, floating-point and quantization errors, and exercises with audio and image processing applications.

courses.name: Project
courses.purpose: This course aims to guide students through the selection, planning, and completion of a computer engineering project under faculty supervision. It also helps students develop independent research, design, and implementation skills.
courses.description: Students read project descriptions published by CmpE project advisors, discuss potential projects with advisors, choose a project, complete the registration form, obtain advisor approval, and register for CMPE 491/492. The course is a project-based senior-level course with no laboratory component.
resources: Project descriptions published by CmpE project advisors
